seo.title: Coding Agent Instruction Files Toolkit
seo.description: Official guidance and a practical template for AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, and GitHub Copilot repository instructions that coding agents can follow.

content.headline: Give coding agents repository instructions they can verify
content.description: Build concise instruction files that explain commands, boundaries, architecture, and verification without duplicating the entire README.

examples.label: Minimal root instruction file

input.template:
# Project
Astro static site; JSON content is schema-validated.

## Commands
- Install: npm ci
- Validate: npm run validate
- Build: npm run build

## Boundaries
- Never commit secrets.
- Keep content slugs stable.
- Do not change deployment files unless requested.

examples.expected_output: An agent can identify the stack, run exact checks, and avoid the highest-risk changes without searching through unrelated documentation.

examples.label: Nested instruction file

input.template:
# Payments
- Preserve webhook idempotency.
- Verify signatures before parsing business fields.
- Run: npm test -- payments
- Use fixtures; never call live payment APIs from tests.

examples.expected_output: The narrower rules apply where payment code is edited, while root-level project rules remain available.

examples.label: Instruction quality check

input.questions: Are commands copy-pasteable? Are risky directories named? Are rules testable? Are contradictions resolved? Is stale product detail linked instead of copied?

examples.expected_output: Every important instruction is specific enough for a contributor to act on and short enough to stay current.

content.how_it_works: Start with the root file recognized by your coding agent, then add narrower files only where a subdirectory has genuinely different rules. State the architecture in two or three sentences, provide exact install/test/build commands, identify generated or security-sensitive paths, and describe what a completed change must verify. Prefer links to canonical design documents over copied explanations. Test the file by giving an agent a small task in a fresh session: if it cannot discover the right command or boundary, revise the instruction. If several agent products are used, keep shared rules aligned and reserve product-specific files for capabilities that truly differ.
